coredata.dc:title: A novel achiral fluorescent nanoprobe for the chiral recognition of cysteine enantiomers
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Anli
Zhong, Yujia
Yin, Xinghang
Li, Renjie
Deng, Qunfen
Yang, Rui
coredata.dc:description: Chiral recognition is of great significance in the field of life science and chemistry. The design and synthesis of chiral nanomaterials have become an important way to realize chiral recognition, while the application of non-chiral materials in this regard is still rare. Here, synthesis of achiral carbon dots (Asn-CDs) using racemate (DL-Asn, Asn) as nitrogen source is proposed, and chiral recognition of cysteine enantiomers (L-/D-Cys) is achieved by employing metal Co2+ as the inducible factor to co-structure a fluorescent probe (Asn-CDs+Co2+). And then, based on the ability of Co2+ to quench the fluorescence of Asn-CDs and L-cysteine (L-Cys) could restore the fluorescence of the Asn-CDs+Co2+ probe, so achieve an “on-off-on” detection mode to detect L-Cys. The Asn-CDs+Co2+ probe can distinguish cysteine enantiomers not only by fluorescence spectra but also by circular dichroism spectra. The probe can be used for the quantitative detection of L-Cys by fluorescence method. Under the optimal conditions, the linear range of L-Cys detection is 1.824–625.0 μM, and the detection limit is 0.6081 μM. The method has the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity, a wide linear range, and fast response. This work may provide a new idea for chiral analysis based on achiral materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chiral recognition
L-/D-cysteine
Achiral nanomaterials
Cobalt ion
“On-off-on” detection mode

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor highlightsabst primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Chiral recognition of Cys and measurement of L-Cys
Chiral recognition of Cys by fluorescence spectra and CD spectra of Asn-CDs
Measurement of L-Cys by Asn-CDs+Co2+ with the fluorescence method
Evaluation of the selectivity and interference experiment
Detection of L-Cys in actual samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Natural Science Foundation Project of Chongqing

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Program for Chongqing Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was financially supported by the Program for Chongqing Natural Science Foundation of China ( cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0489 ). Special thanks to the group of Professor Jiang Yunbao of Xiamen University for their support of CD spectroscopy analysis, and the group of Professor Li Ming of Southwest University for their support of FL lifetime measurements. Thanks to eceshi (www.eceshi.cn) for the help with the TEM analysis, and Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for XPS analysis.

This research was financially supported by the Program for Chongqing Natural Science Foundation of China (cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0489). Special thanks to the group of Professor Jiang Yunbao of Xiamen University for their support of CD spectroscopy analysis, and the group of Professor Li Ming of Southwest University for their support of FL lifetime measurements. Thanks to eceshi (www.eceshi.cn) for the help with the TEM analysis, and Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for XPS analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Image of Asn-CDs:(A) TEM: (a) HRTEM and (b) Lattice distance distribution. (B) Particle size distribution diagram. (C) XRD. (D) FTIR. (E) XPS: (F)C1s, (G)N1s, and (H)O1s.

(A) The normalized spectra of the UV–visible spectrum, excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of Asn-CDs. (B) The photos of the Asn-CDs solution were taken in the sunlight and under the ultraviolet lamp. (C) 3D fluorescence spectra of Asn-CDs obtained under different excitation (330–500 nm). (D) Fluorescence spectra of Asn-CDs, Asn-CDs+L-Cys, Asn-CDs+D-Cys, Asn-CDs+Co2+, Asn-CDs+Co2++L-Cys, Asn-CDs+Co2++D-Cys. (E) Effects of Asn-CDs+ Co2+ on different enantiomers (Cys, Asp, His, Glu, Arg, Ala, Thr, Pro, Asn, AA, Maltose, TA, Gal, Val, Ser, Ile, Gln, Tyr, Met, Leu, Lys, Trp and Phe). (F) Circular dichroic spectra of Asn-CDs, Asn-CDs+Co2+, L-Cys, Asn-CDs+Co2++L-Cys, D-Cys, Asn-CDs + Co2+ +D-Cys, and the inset were local magnification of Asn-CDs and Asn-CDs+Co2+ curves.

(A) Fluorescence response spectra of Asn-CDs+Co2+ system with different concentrations of L-Cys (0–625 μM) (B) Relationship of △F L versus different concentrations of L-Cys(0–1250 μM) (C) Linear relationship between △F L and different concentrations of L-Cys(1.824 – 625.0 μM) (D) Relationship of △F D versus different concentrations of D-Cys(0–875 μM).

(A) Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of Asn-CDs, Asn-CDs+Co2+, Asn-CDs+Co2++L-Cys and Asn-CDs+Co2++D-Cys. (B) Stern-Volmer plots of the Asn-CDs+Co2+ system at different temperatures (288 K, 303 K and 318 K). (C) UV–vis spectra of Asn-CDs, L-Cys and Asn-CDs+L-Cys, the illustration was a local magnification. (D) UV–vis spectra of Asn-CDs, D-Cys and Asn-CDs+D-Cys, the illustration was a local magnification. (E) Circular dichroism spectrum of L-Cys, Asn-CDs+L-Cys, D-Cys and Asn-CDs+D-Cys. (F) Zeta potential of Asn-CDs, Asn-CDs+Co2+, L-Cys, D-Cys, Asn-CDs+Co2++L-Cys and Asn-CDs+Co2++D-Cys.

(A) Use different metal ions (Co2+, Na+, K+, Ag+, Zn2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Pd2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Fe3+ and Hg2+) as the medium to detect the response of L-Cys. (B) Stability of Asn-CDs+Co2++L-Cys on a system of different coexisting substances (0: Blank; 1: DA; 2: Phe; 3: Hypoxanthine; 4: NADP Ⅰ; 5: VB1; 6: GSH; 7: Thymine; 8: Creatinine;9: Sucrose; 10: Glu; 11: 6-Mercaptopurine; 12: Gly; 13: Carnine; 14: Adenine; 15: Proline; 16: K+; 17: I-; 18: Zn2+; 19: SO4 2-; 20: Ca2+; 21: Cl-; 22: Thr; 23: L-Ala; 24: D-Ala).
(A)The preparation process of Asn-CDs. (B) Through the synergistic effect of Asn-CDs and Co2+, the cysteine enantiomers’ identification and detection mechanism are realized.
Analytical results of L-Cys in real samples. (n = 5).

head.ce:title: A novel achiral fluorescent nanoprobe for the chiral recognition of cysteine enantiomers

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, No.2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, People’s Republic of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Southwest University, No.2 Tiansheng Road, Chongqing 400715, People’s Republic of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Chiral recognition is of great significance in the field of life science and chemistry. The design and synthesis of chiral nanomaterials have become an important way to realize chiral recognition, while the application of non-chiral materials in this regard is still rare. Here, synthesis of achiral carbon dots (Asn-CDs) using racemate (DL-Asn, Asn) as nitrogen source is proposed, and chiral recognition of cysteine enantiomers (L-/D-Cys) is achieved by employing metal Co2+ as the inducible factor to co-structure a fluorescent probe (Asn-CDs+Co2+). And then, based on the ability of Co2+ to quench the fluorescence of Asn-CDs and L-cysteine (L-Cys) could restore the fluorescence of the Asn-CDs+Co2+ probe, so achieve an “on-off-on” detection mode to detect L-Cys. The Asn-CDs+Co2+ probe can distinguish cysteine enantiomers not only by fluorescence spectra but also by circular dichroism spectra. The probe can be used for the quantitative detection of L-Cys by fluorescence method. Under the optimal conditions, the linear range of L-Cys detection is 1.824–625.0 μM, and the detection limit is 0.6081 μM. The method has the advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity, a wide linear range, and fast response. This work may provide a new idea for chiral analysis based on achiral materials.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chirality is one of the basic characteristics of biology. Most bioactive substances are chiral, and these chiral molecules show significant differences in activity and toxicity in many life activities, such as metabolism and transport [1,2]. Thus, the realization of chiral recognition of enantiomer is of great significance in the field of analysis and detection [3]. Amino acids are recognized as representative chiral compounds in nature. It is widely believed that L-amino acids are natural components of biological systems, while D-amino acids may show toxicity or metabolic abnormalities [4–6], and can also be used as an indicator of bacterial contamination in food. Therefore, the chiral recognition of amino acids is an important and challenging subject. So far, there has been a lot of excellent work, among which the design and preparation of chiral nanomaterials is an important way to realize the chiral recognition of amino acids. These chiral nanomaterials mainly include chiral precious metals, such as gold nanoparticles with inherent chirality [7], chiral semiconductor materials [8], chiral magnetic nanomaterials [9], and supramolecular gels [10], and many methods for chiral recognition of amino acids have been established [5].

L-cysteine (L-Cys) is a natural protein-derived amino acid, which plays a key role in protein synthesis, cytotoxic detoxification, and various metabolic processes [11]. If L-Cys is lacking in the human body, psoriasis, leukopenia, and hematopoietic function decline will occur [12,13], while excessive D-cysteine (D-Cys) may be closely related to neurological diseases such as Parkinson's disease and Alzheimer's disease [14,15]. Developing new methods for chirality recognition of Cys that are efficient, accurate, and fast is very important. The existing Cys detection methods include the electrochemical method [16], the colorimetric method [17], surface-enhanced Raman scattering (SERS) and surface-assisted laser desorption-ionization mass spectrometry [18], and fluorescence spectroscopy [19]. Among them, fluorescence spectroscopy has been widely concerned because of its simple operation advantages, fast response, high sensitivity, and stable signal. Therefore, the detection and recognition of cysteine by fluorescence technology are still of great significance.

Here, we report a simple and effective strategy for the chiral recognition of cysteine by fluorescence spectra and circular dichroism spectra based on an achiral carbon nanomaterial. The non-chiral carbon dots (Asn-CDs) was synthesized by the solvent-free one-step method using citric acid monohydrate (CA) as the carbon source and racemic asparagine (Asn) as the nitrogen source. As shown in Scheme 1, using the achiral Asn-CDs as the probe and Co2+ as the inducible factor, the chiral recognition of Cys can be realized by fluorescence spectra. It is found that Co2+ can effectively quench the fluorescence of Asn-CDs, and the selective binding of L-Cys and Asn-CDs prevents the collision of Co2+ on excited Asn-CDs, thus restoring the fluorescence of Asn-CDs to realize the detection of L-Cys. However, D-Cys has no significant effect on the fluorescence of Asn-CDs+Co2+. Interestingly, the circular dichroic spectra of Asn-CDs+Co2+ also show different responses to L-Cys and D-Cys. The selectivity and anti-interference abilities of the achiral fluorescence probe were evaluated, and the determination of L-Cys in human serum, milk, and tap water samples were completed, wherein the concentration of L-Cys was about 250 μM in the total human plasma [20]. The chiral recognition of Cys by using achiral carbon dots provides more directions and approaches for the research of new materials for chiral recognition.

Citric acid monohydrate (CA) was purchased from Beijing Chemical Reagent Company. Asparagine (DL-Asn, Asn), L-/D-cysteine (L-/D-Cys), and other amino acids were purchased from Boyun Biotechnology Co., LTD (Shanghai, China), Xushuo Biotechnology Co., LTD (Shanghai, China), Biode Medical Technology Co., LTD, and Darai Fine Chemicals Co., LTD (Shanghai, China). CoH12N2O12 was purchased from Aladdin Reagents (Shanghai, China). Other drugs were purchased from Aladdin Reagent (Shanghai, China), Yuexiang Chemical Co., LTD (Chongqing, China), and McLean Biochemical Technology Co., LTD (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used directly without further purification. Microporous membrane filter aperture 0.22 µm was purchased from Jinteng Experimental Equipment Co., LTD (Tianjin, China). Deionized water was used in all experiments.

UV–vis absorption and fluorescence (FL) spectra were recorded using a UV-2700 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and an FS5 FL spectrophotometer (Edinburgh, Britain), respectively. Circular dichroism spectra (CD) were obtained using a J-810 spectropolarimeter (JASCO Corporation, Japan). FL lifetime measurements of samples were carried out on FL-TCSPC Fluorolog-3 (Horiba, USA). Transmission electron microscope (TEM) and High-resolution TEM images (HR-TEM) were obtained with a JEOL JEM-2100F transmission electron microscope (Shimadzu, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed on a SCIENTIFIC ESCALAB 250Xi Multi-technique Surface Analysis (Thermo, USA). The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was measured by an FT-IR 8400S spectrometer (Bruker, Germany). A SHIMADZU 7000 (Beijing Spectrum Analysis General, China) was supplied for the X-ray diffraction (XRD) spectra of CDs. The pH values (pH=2–12) were investigated by a PB-10 pH meter (Beijing Sartorius Instrument Company, Ltd., China).

According to the different conditions for synthesizing Asn-CDs, such as the 3rd section of the Supplementary information as shown in Fig. S1, the proportion of raw materials, synthesis temperature and reaction time were adjusted. Finally, the ratio of citric acid (CA) to asparagine (Asn) was 1:3, the synthesis temperature was 180 ℃, and the reaction time was 4 h. 1.0 g of CA monohydrate and 1.9 g of Asn were evenly mixed by a pressure agitator and transferred to the Teflon-lined stainless autoclave (50 mL), which was placed into the sealed autoclave vessels of an electric oven at 180 ℃ for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, 5 mL deionized water was added to the product and ultrasonic for 10 min to make it fully dissolved. After centrifugation for 10 min at a rotating speed of 8000 r·min-1, carbon dots (Asn-CDs) stock solution was obtained by filtration with 0.22 µm drainage membrane. Asn-CDs were obtained by eluting the stock solution with a large amount of acetone, clarifying the supernatant and then drying the precipitated products to powder in a drying oven at 65 ℃.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chiral recognition of Cys and measurement of L-Cys
ce:section.ce:para: Considering the solubility of L-/D-Cys, 0.1 M hydrochloric acid was used to dissolve the substance and deionized water was added to form L-/D-Cys with a concentration of 0.1 M as the bulk solution, which was further applied in the detection of subsequent experiments. 0.05 mL BR buffer solution (Britton-Robinson Buffer Solution, pH = 6.5), 0.2 mL Asn-CDs (11.6 μg·mL-1) and 20 μL Co2+ (0.1 M) were mixed evenly into a 5.0 mL colorimetric tube. Then 0.1 mL L-/D-Cys solution was added and diluted to 2.0 mL with deionized water. Thus, the ratio for the reaction volume of Asn-CDs+Co2+ with L-Cys was 27:10. After shaking and reaction at room temperature for 2 min, fluorescence spectra (excitation wavelength was 372 nm) and circular dichroism (CD) spectra were detected. Under the above conditions, the working curves of L-Cys were measured by fluorescence spectrometry, and the actual samples were measured by the recovery method.

As shown in Fig. 1A, Asn-CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and had a quasi-spherical structure and good water dispersion [21]. According to statistical data on particle size, which distribution was between 2.0 and 4.0 nm, and its average diameter was 2.8 nm ± 0.4 nm (Fig. 1B). Combined with the HRTEM image (Fig. 1a) and lattice analysis diagram (Fig. 1b), lattice fringes with Asn-CDs plane spacing of 0.21 nm can be observed [22]. Fig. 1C, an X-ray diffraction diagram, represented the crystal characteristics of Asn-CDs, which showed Asn-CDs with a wide diffraction peak at about 26.5°, corresponding to the typical graphite structure [23].

FTIR was used to explore the functional groups on the surface of Asn-CDs. As shown in Fig. 1D, there were abundant oxygen-containing functional groups. For example, the absorption peak at 3408 cm-1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of the O-H bond, and the stretching vibration of the CO and C-O bonds were at 1705 cm-1 and 1193 cm-1. The wide absorption peak at 3196 cm-1 was considered to N-H bond, the C-N bond was at 1399 cm-1, and a strong signal about 1589 cm-1 was defined as the stretching vibration of CC [24,25]. The data showed that Asn-CDs preserved the functional groups of the raw materials, and were composed of the sp2 conjugated domain of the aromatic structure and the surface groups containing oxygen and nitrogen.

As shown in Fig. 1E, XPS was adopted to measure and analyze the element content and surface structures of Asn-CDs, which proved Asn-CDs were composed of C(40.47 %), N(13.50 %) and O(46.03 %) elements, respectively [26]. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s was fitted into four Gaussian peaks (Fig. 1F). The peak at 284.2 eV was attributed to CC/C-C, the peaks at 285.2 and 286.2 eV were C-N and C-O, respectively, and at 287.6 eV was the position of CO binding energy. To describe in detail the existence of nitrogen (Fig. 1G), three Gauss peaks near 399.15 eV (pyridine nitrogen) and 399.8 eV (amino nitrogen) and 400.8 eV (pyrrole nitrogen) were obtained by fitting the high-resolution XPS data of N1s. And the proportion of graphite nitrogen could not be fitted basically, which may be one of the reasons why Asn-CDs cannot emit fluorescence in the long wavelength part [27,28]. However, high-resolution O1s XPS spectra (Fig. 1H) can fit data peaks at the binding energies of 531.2 eV (CO) and 532.7 eV (C-O)[27]. Detailed group analysis results of the samples were shown in Table S1. It could be seen that Asn-CDs retained most of the structure of raw materials and corresponded to FTIR, providing response sites for further application, analysis and detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Chiral recognition of Cys by fluorescence spectra and CD spectra of Asn-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence emission peak of Asn-CDs was located at 450 nm[29] (λ ex = 372 nm, pH = 6.5, Fig. 2A), the maximum absorption peak was located at 337 nm, and bright blue fluorescence could be observed under ultraviolet light (Fig. 2B). And the quantum yield of Asn-CDs was 48.5 %. When the excitation wavelength changed from 330 to 500 nm, the emission center was almost unchanged (Fig. 2C), showing a typical excitation-independent FL behavior [30].

As shown in Fig. 2D, when L-Cys and D-Cys are added into Asn-CDs respectively, the fluorescence intensity decreases slightly. Therefore, Cys cannot be directly identified and detected by Asn-CDs. While Co2+ was introduced into Asn-CDs, the fluorescence at 450 nm was significantly quenching, and the relationship between Asn-CDs and Co2+ of the fluorescence quenching degree was shown in Fig. S2. At this time, if L-Cys and D-Cys were added separately, the changes in fluorescence signals would undergo significant differences. The addition of D-Cys further slightly weakened the fluorescence of Asn-CDs+Co2+, while L-Cys led to significant fluorescence enhancement. To evaluate the enantioselectivity of the Asn-CDs+Co2+ probe for amino acids, the fluorescence enhancement ratio ef, was determined [31–33], (ef = ΔF L/ΔF D = [F L – F 0] / [ F D− F 0], where F 0 was the fluorescence intensity of Asn-CDs+Co2+ probe, F L or F D were the fluorescence intensity after adding L- or D- amino acids into Asn-CDs+Co2+, respectively), and the results were shown in Fig. 2 E. It was noteworthy that, the ef values of the enantiomers of other amino acids and other enantiomers (such as Asp, His, Glu, Arg, Ala, Thr, Pro, Asn, AA, Maltose, TA, Gal, Val, Ser, Ile, Gln, Tyr, Met, Leu, Lys, Trp and Phe) were very small, meanwhile, only Cys had a large ef value, suggesting that the Asn-CDs+Co2+ probe could selectively recognize L-/D-Cys [34].

The CD spectra also showed the application of achiral material Asn-CDs in the chiral recognition of Cys. As could be seen from Fig. 2F, Asn-CDs had no Cotton signal and were a typical achiral carbon nanomaterial. With the addition of Co2+, a wide range (190–232 nm) of negative Cotton signal and a weak positive Cotton signal at 236 nm was observed, simultaneously. At this point, after adding L-Cys or D-Cys, as shown in Fig. 2F, obvious differences in CD signals could be observed: The original positive Cotton signal at 220 nm of L-Cys disappeared and produced a strong negative Cotton signal near 265 nm, while the original negative Cotton signal at 220 nm of D-Cys disappeared and the positive Cotton signal at 260 nm was enhanced. This change of CD signal could be observed without adding a high concentration of Cys, indicating that using Co2+ as the inducible factor, non-chiral nanomaterial Asn-CDs could also perform sensitive chiral recognition of Cys through CD spectra.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of L-Cys by Asn-CDs+Co2+ with the fluorescence method
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the phenomenon of fluorescence recovery after adding L-Cys into the Asn-CDs+Co2+ probe (Fig. 2D), we designed a fluorescence spectrometric method to measure L-Cys. Firstly, the stability of Asn-CDs under different conditions (Fig. S3) was investigated (such as pH, temperature, NaCl concentration, illumination, storage time, and in different solvents), and it could be seen that Asn-CDs had good optical stability. Then, the influences of experimental conditions such as pH, temperature, amount of Co2+, and reaction time on the test results were tested. Specific test results were shown in Fig. S4. Finally, the optimal reaction conditions were determined as pH= 6.5, the reaction temperature at room temperature, the volume of Co2+ in the system was 20 μL, and the reaction time was 2 min. Under the optimal experimental conditions, the working curve, linear response range, and detection limit of the Asn-CDs+Co2+ sensor system for the detection of L-Cys were determined. Fig. 3A was the fluorescence spectrum of L-Cys detection. It could be seen that the fluorescence around 450 nm enhanced gradually at L-Cys concentrations ranging from 0 μM - 625 μM. However, the fluorescence recovery value (△F L) increased first and tended to be flat finally at L-Cys concentrations ranging from 0 μM - 1250 μM. (Fig. 3B).

Furthermore, the linear fitting equation was △F L = 147.5C L - 1078, (R²= 0.9983) (Fig. 3C), here, △F L = F L – F 0, F 0 and F L were the fluorescence intensity of Asn-CDs+Co2+ before and after the addition of L-Cys, respectively. The linear range was 1.824–625.0 μM, and the detection limit (LOD) was calculated to be 0.6081 μM. Compared with other methods of L-Cys detection in the reported literature, the linear range of this work was significantly wider, and the detection limit was also relatively low. If the Asn-CDs+Co2+ probe was used to detect D-Cys by fluorescence method, as shown in Fig. 3D, the fluorescence intensity of the probe decreased after the addition of D-Cys. However, the change in fluorescence intensity was not obvious enough and did not show a linear relationship. According to the actual situation, it is indeed common for L-Cys and D-Cys to coexist in the same sample, so we have also conducted detection experiments on the coexistence of L-Cys and D-Cys. The experimental data as shown in Fig. S5 indicated that with the constant increase of the enantiomeric percentage of L-Cys. the overall trend of fluorescence recovery was to decrease first and then increase, which was not linear relation. Therefore, quantitative detection of cysteine isomer mixture cannot be carried out and the fluorescent probe Asn-CDs+Co2+ was only used to detect L-Cys.

The emission mechanism of Asn-CDs was discussed in S6. Since the absorption band of Co2+ at 309 nm did not overlap with the excitation peak of Asn-CDs at 372 nm (Fig. S7), this meant that the fluorescence quenching of Asn-CDs caused by the addition of Co2+ could not be attributed to internal filtering effect (IFE) [35–37] or fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [38–40]. Meanwhile, the addition of Co2+ did not significantly change the absorption band of Asn-CDs (Fig. S7), indicating that a ground state complex did not form thus static quenching had not occurred. Noteworthy, the fluorescence lifetime of Asn-CDs and Asn-CDs+Co2+ was 6.43 ns and 3.26 ns respectively, as shown in Fig. 4 A and Table S3. Then, Asn-CDs+Co2+ system of temperature change experiments were conducted, and it was found that the Stern-Volmer constant (K SV, S 2.5) of Co2+ to Asn-CDs at three temperatures (288 K, 303 K and 318 K) increased with the increase in temperature (Fig. 4B). Therefore, it could be speculated that fluorescence quenching was caused by the interaction of Co2+ with excited Asn-CDs, which belonged to the dynamic quenching process (DQE) [41,42].

When L-Cys or D-Cys were added to Asn-CDs+Co2+, the fluorescence lifetime was 5.28 ns and 2.75 ns, respectively (Fig. 4A and Table S3), suggesting that only the addition of L-Cys inhibited the collision of Co2+ to excited Asn-CDs. According to the ultraviolet-visible spectrum, it could be seen that neither L-Cys nor D-Cys combined with Co2+(Fig. S8 and Fig. S9). However, L-Cys could combine with Asn-CDs, resulting in changes in its absorption spectra, while D-Cys did not combine with Asn-CDs (Fig. 4 C and D). Therefore, it could be inferred that the combination of L-Cys and Asn-CDs reduced the collision probability of Co2+ on excited Asn-CDs [43], resulting in enhanced fluorescence of Asn-CDs+Co2+. Nevertheless, D-Cys did not bind with Asn-CDs in the ground state, and the addition of D-Cys further reduced the fluorescence lifetime, so the fluorescence intensity continued to decrease.

To explain the chiral recognition function of the Asn-CDs+Co2+ system, the circular dichroism spectrum was tested and the luminescence asymmetry factor (g factor) was calculated for this system (S 2.6). The g factor was an index to judge the luminescence ability of different chiral molecules in circular polarization[44], and its circular dichroism was evaluated by the absolute value of the value. As shown in Fig. 2F, Asn-CDs had no Cotton signal, and its g value is 0. The asymmetric carbon centers of L-/D-Cys provided chiral geometric structures, with g values of (+) 2.87 and (-) 3.08, respectively. If Asn-CDs were directly mixed with L-/D-Cys, the CD spectra were shown in Fig. 4E. When Asn-CDs and L-/D-Cys coexisted, the phenomenon rule was similar, and the g values of Asn-CDs+L-/D-Cys were (-) 5.76 and (-) 3.71, so L/D-Cys cannot be recognized by a single Asn-CDs. It was speculated that Asn-CDs could effectively combine with L-Cys, leading to a significant increase in their asymmetry, while Asn-CDs and D-Cys almost did not combine, and the change of g value was not obvious. After Co2+ was added to the Asn-CDs solution, the g value was (-) 2.15. It was believed that the optical activity of Asn-CDs was enhanced by the collision of Co2+ in the excited state, which destroyed its inherent symmetry and endowed the detection system with hybrid optical activity[45]. This indicated that the Cotton signal generated by Co2+ induced achiral nanomaterials might be due to the adjustment of the nanoscale environment [46], which was quite different from the known coulomb force between noble metal ions and chiral nanomaterials to generate plasma elementary enhanced CD signal and was also the key to the application of the nanoparticle regulation strategy for chiral recognition. When L-Cys was added to Asn-CDs+Co2+ (g value was (-) 1.06), the collision of Co2+ on Asn-CDs was prevented, which may due to be the combination of L-Cys and Asn-CDs, resulting in reducing asymmetry in the microenvironment. At the same time, the presence of Co2+ weakened the asymmetry of the Asn-CDs+Co2++L-Cys system, and the g value changed from (-) 5.76 to (-) 1.06. However, when D-Cys was added to Asn-CDs+Co2+, its luminescence asymmetry factor increased to (+) 4.47, higher than the g value of Asn-CDs+D-Cys, which may be due to the increase in asymmetry caused by collisions between the three types of particles. By synthesizing the changes of the three samples in the CD spectrum, when Co2+ was used as the inducible factor, the chiral recognition of Asn-CDs+Co2+ on L-Cys was the result of synergy.

To further investigated the interaction between Asn-CDs, Co2+, and L-Cys or D-Cys, Zeta potential was tested, and the results were shown in Fig. 4F. When pH= 6.5, the Zeta potential of Asn-CDs and Asn-CDs+Co2+ were −16.40 mV and −6.44 mV, respectively, indicating that the electrostatic attraction between Asn-CDs and Co2+ might cause electron transfer [47]. This electrostatic attraction might be intensified in excited states, resulting in violent collisions, which quenched the fluorescence of Asn-CDs. After the addition of positively charged L-Cys (+5.40 mV) and D-Cys (+4.60 mV), the Zeta potential changed to −12.60 mV and −5.81 mV, respectively, suggesting that the binding of L-Cys and Asn-CDs prevented the interaction between Co2+ and Asn-CDs. However, D-Cys did not bind with Asn-CDs, so the interaction between Co2+ and Asn-CDs had no effect. Therefore, L-Cys enhanced the fluorescence of Asn-CDs+Co2+, while D-Cys further weakened the fluorescence. The change of Zeta potential revealed that L-Cys and D-Cys did have different action modes with Asn-CDs+Co2+ systems, therefore, fluorescence spectra and CD spectra could be used for chiral recognition of Cys enantiomers.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the selectivity and interference experiment
ce:section.ce:para: According to the previous amino acid enantioselectivity experiment (Fig. 2E), the Asn-CDs+Co2+ probe showed high selectivity for chiral recognition of Cys enantiomers. To further investigate the reliability of the probe in the measurement of L-Cys, different metal ions Mn+ were investigated as the inducible factor, where the concentration of Co2+ and other Mn+ was both 1.0 mM, as shown in Fig. 5A. F 1 and F 2 represented the fluorescence intensity before and after the addition of L-Cys to the Asn-CDs+Mn+ system. The results showed that the fluorescence was enhanced only after the addition of L-Cys to Asn-CDs+Co2+, while the fluorescence in other Asn-CDs+Mn+ systems was further quenched.

To evaluate the anti-interference performance of the Asn-CDs+Co2+ system in detecting L-Cys, the fluorescence response of the Asn-CDs+Co2++L-Cys system toward various coexisting materials was investigated, and the results were shown in Fig. 5B. It could be seen that most of the co-existing substances had little interference on the detection of L-Cys by the Asn-CDs+Co2+ system, indicating that the system had a strong anti-interference ability and was expected to be used for L-Cys measurement in practical samples with complex composition.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of L-Cys in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: Different actual samples (including human serum, milk, and water samples) were used for the L-Cys determination, and the accuracy of the method was checked by the standard addition method. Fasting human serum samples provided by young and healthy volunteers were selected, the serum was centrifuged again, the supernatant was taken, and human serum diluted 100 times was used for detection. Take a small amount of drinkable milk diluted 750 times until clear for testing. Tap water instead of distilled water was used as a diluent for actual sample testing, and the test results were shown in Table 1 . The recoveries of L-Cys were 100.02–105.04 % and the relative standard deviations were 0.61–2.48 %. The above results show that the method has high accuracy and repeatability, and the detection results are satisfactory.

In summary, using citric acid and asparagine as precursors, novel achiral blue carbon dots (Asn-CDs) were synthesized by a solvent-free one-step method. In the presence of Co2+, both fluorescence spectroscopy and circular dichroism spectroscopy of Asn-CDs could be used for chiral identification of L-/D-Cys, while other amino acid enantiomers could not be recognized. Meanwhile, Co2+ could not be replaced by other metal ions to realize chiral recognition of Cys. Employing Asn-CDs+Co2+ as a probe, L-Cys could be determined by fluorescence spectrometry with high selectivity and sensitivity. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report on the determination of chiral amino acids based on achiral Asn-CDs interacting synergistically with Co2+. This study provides a new strategy for the application of achiral nanomaterials in the realization of chiral recognition.

An-Li Chen: Conceptualization; Data curation; Formal analysis; Investigation; Writing - original draft; Writing - review & editing. Yu-Jia Zhong: Formal analysis; Investigation. Xing-Hang Yin: Conceptualization; Investigation. Ren-Jie Li: Data curation; Investigation. Qun-Fen Deng: Data curation; Investigation. Rui Yang: Funding acquisition; Resources; Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts of interest in this study.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was financially supported by the Program for Chongqing Natural Science Foundation of China (cstc2019jcyj-msxmX0489). Special thanks to the group of Professor Jiang Yunbao of Xiamen University for their support of CD spectroscopy analysis, and the group of Professor Li Ming of Southwest University for their support of FL lifetime measurements. Thanks to eceshi (www.eceshi.cn) for the help with the TEM analysis, and Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for XPS analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2023.134262.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
